---
title: "Regression Discontinuity"
subtitle: "Causal effects at a threshold"
---

## Overview

Many policies switch on at a sharp cutoff: a patient becomes eligible for a screening program at age 70; a vårdcentral receives extra funding when an area's deprivation index crosses a line; a subsidy kicks in above an income threshold. Regression discontinuity (RD) designs turn these arbitrary thresholds into natural experiments.

The logic is disarmingly simple. Units *just* above and *just* below the cutoff are, in every respect except treatment, nearly identical --- so any jump in the outcome at the cutoff can be attributed to the treatment. RD trades external validity for unusually credible internal validity: it estimates the effect precisely at the threshold, where comparability is strongest.

This module covers the sharp design and its identification logic, how to estimate the jump without overfitting, the fuzzy design (where crossing the cutoff only shifts the *probability* of treatment, making RD a form of IV), the falsification tests that defend an RD, and how to interpret --- and not over-extrapolate --- a local effect.

## Pages in This Module

- [Sharp Regression Discontinuity](sharp-rd.qmd) --- Identification at a cutoff.
- [Estimation and Bandwidth](estimation-bandwidth.qmd) --- Local polynomials, kernels, and the bias-variance trade-off.
- [Fuzzy Regression Discontinuity](fuzzy-rd.qmd) --- Imperfect compliance and RD as instrumental variables.
- [Validity and Falsification](validity-tests.qmd) --- Manipulation, balance, and placebo tests.
- [Interpretation and External Validity](interpretation.qmd) --- The effect at the cutoff and what it does (and does not) tell you.
